fr:mainmatter.html:p: This includes a description of guarded recursion with presheaves/families on direct categories. Notably, well-founded orders form direct categories, and so this recovers the notion of guarded recursion found in the topos of trees. We give a description of all the components that go into this construction, where they may be found at in the code, and then some examples that use it.

fr:title.text: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}. Write \varepsilon : F \circ U \Rightarrow \mathsf {Id} for the counit of the adjunction. Every object d of \mathcal {D} then induces a T-algebra carried by the object U d, witnessed by the map U \varepsilon _{d} : U F U d \to U d.
This assignment extends to a functor into the Eilenberg–Moore category, K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, the comparison functor of the adjunction.

Dually, an adjunction induces a comonad on the other side and a comparison into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category. When these comparisons are equivalences we say that the adjunction F \dashv U is (co)monadic.

An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}, and a comparison functor K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} sending each object of \mathcal {D} to the T-algebra it carries.
The functor U is monadic when K is an equivalence: the adjunction exhibits \mathcal {D} as objects of \mathcal {C} equipped with algebraic structure for T, the Eilenberg–Moore category.

Comonadicity is monadicity in the opposite category: a left adjoint L : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} with right adjoint R induces a comonad W = L \circ R on \mathcal {C}, a comparison \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category, and L is comonadic when this comparison is an equivalence.

fr:title.text: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The adjoint triple \mathrm {Free} \dashv U \dashv \mathrm {Cofree} induces a monad T = U \circ \mathrm {Free} and a comonad W = U \circ \mathrm {Cofree} on \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}.

Both comparison functors are equivalences: presheaves are the Eilenberg–Moore algebras of T and the co-Eilenberg–Moore coalgebras of W, \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} \qquad \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}. So presheaves are both monadic and comonadic over families.

Reading the algebra structure concretely: a T-algebra on a family A is a map \sum _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , y\right )\mathclose {}} \times A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each x, subject to the monad algebra laws — that is, exactly a functorial action of restriction.

The comonadic reading is the same structure seen from the element's side: a W-coalgebra is a map A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \to \prod _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (y , x\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, giving each value its restriction along every morphism into x. Where the monad says restriction acts on values, the comonad says a value already carries all of its restrictions — and the coalgebra laws say it does so coherently.

fr:title.text: The adjoint triple between presheaves and families

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The adjoint triple between presheaves and families

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A family over \mathcal {C} is a set A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each object x, with no action of morphisms. Families form a category \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}: a morphism A \to B is a function A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \to B{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each x.

The two adjoints demonstrate different means of forcing a family to be functorial. The right adjoint universally quantifies over morphisms in, \mathrm {Cofree}{\mathopen {}\left (A\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \prod _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (y , x\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, with restriction along f given by precomposition. The left adjoint instead existentially quantifiers over morphisms out: \mathrm {Free}{\mathopen {}\left (A\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \sum _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , y\right )\mathclose {}} \times A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, with restriction acting on the first component. (For \mathrm {Free} we ask that \mathcal {C} have a set of objects, so that this sum is a set and thus \mathrm {Free} defines a presheaf.)

fr:title.text: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A comonad on \mathcal {C} is a monad W on \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}. Everything about its coalgebras is then inherited from the Eilenberg–Moore construction, instantiated at the opposite category — nothing is defined twice.

Algebras of W over \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} are coalgebras \gamma \in \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , W x\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor, and the monad algebra laws, read in \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}, are the comonad coalgebra laws — the unit and multiplication of W, viewed in \mathcal {C}, are the counit \varepsilon and comultiplication \delta : \gamma \star \varepsilon _{x} = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \gamma \star \delta _{x} = \gamma \star W \gamma .

The co-Eilenberg–Moore category is the opposite of the total category: \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (\mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}}.

fr:title.text: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Fix a monad {\mathopen {}\left (T, \eta , \mu \right )\mathclose {}} on \mathcal {C}. Its Eilenberg–Moore category arises in two displayed layers. The first layer is the displayed category of algebras \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor.

The second layer, \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, is displayed over the total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}. Over an algebra {\mathopen {}\left (x, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} the displayed objects are the propositions that \alpha satisfies the monad algebra laws: \eta _{x} \star \alpha = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \mu _{x} \star \alpha = T \alpha \star \alpha .
The Eilenberg–Moore category is the total category of the tower: \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}.

Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. A terminal F-coalgebra, written \nu F, is a terminal object of the category of coalgebras \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} — equivalently, an initial algebra for F^{\mathrm {op}}.

Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. Coalgebras require no new construction: a coalgebra is an algebra in the opposite category. Define \mathrm {CoalgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F^{\mathrm {op}}\right )\mathclose {}}, a displayed category over \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}, where F^{\mathrm {op}} : \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} \to \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} is F acting on the opposite category.
Concretely, over an object x a displayed object is a structure map \gamma \in \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , F x\right )\mathclose {}}.

The category of coalgebras is the opposite of the total category: \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (\int \mathrm {CoalgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}}. The outer opposite returns morphisms to the direction of \mathcal {C}: a morphism {\mathopen {}\left (x, \gamma \right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (y, \delta \right )\mathclose {}} is a map f : x \to y with f \star \delta = \gamma \star F f.
Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. An initial F-algebra, written \mu F, is an initial object of the category of algebras \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}.
Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. The F-algebras form a displayed category \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} over \mathcal {C}.
The total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} is the category of F-algebras.

html:p.html:strong: Direct categories and the guarded modalities.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A predecessor of x is a top element of its strict downset: a strict morphism \rho : p \to x through which every strict morphism into x factors uniquely. Equivalently, \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \mathsf {y}\, p — the downset is representable.

The Yoneda lemma then collapses later to evaluation: \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}},\, P\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {y}\, p,\, P\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (p\right )\mathclose {}}, with \mathsf {next} becoming restriction along \rho . The name is from the naturals: every strict map into n+1 factors through n \to n+1, so on \omega — the topos of trees — later is just the shift \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (0\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \top , \qquad \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, and a Löb step is a base value together with a rule producing the value at n+1 from the value at n.
When this predecessor exists, we can give a simpler description of later, as in the topos of trees, but this may not be possible in all direct categories.
Just as for presheaves, the fixed point constructed above is unique: the two transpositions are bijections, and the presheaf-level fixed point is already unique.

Conjugation with the adjunction between presheaves and families U \dashv \mathrm {Cofree} lets us induce a later construction on families from the one on presheaves, \rhd _{\mathrm {Fam}} = U \circ \rhd \circ \mathrm {Cofree} : \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}.

Write X \Rightarrow Y for the presheaf of morphisms X \to Y. An endofunctor F on presheaves is locally contractive when its action on morphisms factors through later: there is a map F_\delta : \rhd {\mathopen {}\left (X \Rightarrow Y\right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (F X \Rightarrow F Y\right )\mathclose {}}

fr:title.text: Löb induction for presheaves on a direct category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction for presheaves on a direct category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {C} be a direct category and P a presheaf on it. Every map \varphi : \rhd P \to P has a fixed point: a global element \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi : \top \to P with \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi = \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi \star \mathsf {next} \star \varphi , and this fixed point is unique.

The hypothesis says: the value of P at any object is determined by its values over the strict past — \varphi turns a coherent family over the strict downset of x into a value at x. The proof is recursion along the well-founded \prec : at each x, the section already constructed over the past assembles into an element of {\mathopen {}\left (\rhd P\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}, and \varphi extends it to x.

Later takes a limit over smaller indices. Dually, the earlier modality takes a colimit over larger indices: an element of \lhd P at x is a P-element sitting at some object y strictly above x, carried down along a chosen morphism x \to y.
Earlier is left adjoint to later: \lhd \dashv \rhd
Under this adjunction, \mathsf {next} : P \to \rhd P corresponds to \mathsf {prev} : \lhd P \to P,.

The later modality on presheaves is given at each object by mapping out of the strict downset: {\mathopen {}\left (\rhd P\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}},\, P\right )\mathclose {}}. An element of \rhd P at x is a coherent choice of P-elements for all objects strictly smaller than x.

Restriction in \rhd P along f : y \to x precomposes with the induced map \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}. At an object of minimal degree the strict downset is empty, so \rhd P is trivial there.

Via functoriality, every presheaf restricts to smaller indices: the map \mathsf {next} : P \to \rhd P sends an element p over x to the family of all its restrictions along morphisms from strictly lower objects.

fr:title.text: Maximality of the strict downset among proper sieves

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Maximality of the strict downset among proper sieves

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Call a direct structure reflecting when every morphism between objects of equal degree is a split epimorphism. In a reflecting direct category, every non-invertible-in-degree morphism strictly raises degree, and the strict downset is as large as a proper sieve can be:
If the direct structure is reflecting, then every proper sieve S on x refines into the strict downset: S \subseteq \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}.

For suppose S \ni f with f : y \to x of equal degree. By reflection f has a section s, and closure under precomposition gives S \ni s \star f = \mathsf {id}_{x}, contradicting properness. So every member of S strictly raises degree, which is membership in \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: The strict downset sieve of a direct category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The strict downset sieve of a direct category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {C} carry a direct structure. The strict downset \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} of an object x is the presheaf of morphisms into x from strictly lower objects: \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left \{f : y \to x \mid y \prec x\right \}\mathclose {}}, with restriction by precomposition — well defined since degrees are non-decreasing, so precomposing can only stay strictly below.

The evident inclusion \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \rightarrowtail よ\, x makes \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} a sieve on x. It is moreover a proper sieve, as it exlcudes the identity.
The representable \mathsf {y}\, x is itself a sieve on x. A sieve on x is proper when it is not equal to the representable.
Say that a proper sieve is maximal when it contains all other proper sieves as a sub-sieve.

A sieve on an object x of \mathcal {C} is a subobject of the representable presheaf \mathsf {y}\, x: a presheaf S with a monic morphism S \rightarrowtail \mathsf {y}\, x. Sieves are a generalization from the notion of ideal found in ring theory to category theory.

A morphism f : y 	o x belongs to S, written S 
i f, when f lies in the image of the inclusion at y. Because S is a presheaf and the inclusion is natural, membership is closed under precomposition: S 
i f \Rightarrow S 
i g \star f \quad 	ext {for every } g : z 	o y. A sieve is thus a "downward closed" collection of morphisms into x.
Sieves on x are ordered by refinement: S \subseteq T when every morphism belonging to S belongs to T.

fr:title.text: Well-founded posets are thin direct categories

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Well-founded posets are thin direct categories

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every poset forms a thin category. Similarly, if the poset is well-founded then it induces a thin direct category.

A well-founded order is a set D with a proposition-valued transitive relation < admitting no infinite descent: every element is accessible. Write a \le b for {\mathopen {}\left (a < b\right )\mathclose {}} \vee {\mathopen {}\left (a = b\right )\mathclose {}}.

A direct structure on a category \mathcal {C} over {\mathopen {}\left (D, <\right )\mathclose {}} is a functor \mathrm {deg} : \mathcal {C} \to {\mathopen {}\left (D, \le \right )\mathclose {}} into the well-founded order viewed as a poset category. The functor organizes two pieces of data at once: an ordering on the objects, and the invariant that morphisms respect it — f : x \to y forces \mathrm {deg} x \le \mathrm {deg} y.

fr:title.text: Fixed points of locally contractive functors are unique

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Fixed points of locally contractive functors are unique

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let F be a locally contractive endofunctor on presheaves over a direct category, and suppose X is any fixed point of F: an object with an isomorphism \alpha : F X \cong X. Then {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} is an initial algebra \mu F, and simultaneously {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha ^{-1}\right )\mathclose {}} is a terminal coalgebra \nu F.

Both claims follow from every value of the hylomorphism profunctor being a singleton. For initiality of {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}}: a map to an algebra {\mathopen {}\left (B, \beta \right )\mathclose {}} satisfying the initiality square is the same thing as a hylomorphism from the coalgebra {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha ^{-1}\right )\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left (B, \beta \right )\mathclose {}}, and there is exactly one of those. Terminality follows similarly.
Thus for locally contractive F, we have an inital-final coincidence \mu F \;\cong \; \nu F, and guarded domain equations have unique solutions.

fr:title.text: Local contractivity makes every coalgebra recursive

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Local contractivity makes every coalgebra recursive

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If F is a locally contractive endofunctor on presheaves over a direct category, then every value of the profunctor \mathrm {Hylo} is a singleton: every coalgebra is recursive and every algebra is corecursive.

The proof is one application of Löb. A hylomorphism from c to \alpha is a fixed point of h \mapsto c \star F h \star \alpha on the presheaf X \Rightarrow B of morphisms. Local contractivity factors this assignment through \rhd {\mathopen {}\left (X \Rightarrow B\right )\mathclose {}}, so Löb provides a fixed point and provides it uniquely.

Hylomorphisms organize into a profunctor \mathrm {Hylo} : \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}} \times \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathbf {Set}, sending a coalgebra and an algebra to the set of hylomorphisms between them.

A coalgebra is recursive exactly when \mathrm {Hylo}{\mathopen {}\left (c, -\right )\mathclose {}} is constantly a singleton, and an algebra is corecursive exactly when \mathrm {Hylo}{\mathopen {}\left (-, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} is. Saying that all values of \mathrm {Hylo} are singletons says every divide-and-conquer specification over F has a unique solution.

A coalgebra c : X \to F X is recursive when for every algebra \alpha : F B \to B there is exactly one hylomorphism X \to B. Recursion out of X is then total and deterministic: any way of recombining answers yields a uniquely-defined function.

Recursiveness is the coalgebraic form of well-foundedness. The coalgebra decomposes each element into subproblems; recursiveness says this decomposition terminates, without ever mentioning an ordering. Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann develop intrinsically correct algorithms from exactly this notion.

Dually, an algebra \alpha is corecursive when every coalgebra admits exactly one hylomorphism into it. An initial algebra gives a recursive coalgebra (its inverse, by Lambek's lemma), and a terminal coalgebra gives a corecursive algebra.
This is divide and conquer as an equation: c splits a problem into an F-shape of subproblems, F h solves them, and \alpha recombines the answers.

On grammars, the earlier modality is a sum over proper extensions of the input: {\mathopen {}\left (\lhd X\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (v\right )\mathclose {}} = \sum _{w \neq \varepsilon } X\,{\mathopen {}\left (w \cdot v\right )\mathclose {}}. Writing D_w for the Brzozowski derivative, {\mathopen {}\left (D_w B\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (v\right )\mathclose {}} = B\,{\mathopen {}\left (w \cdot v\right )\mathclose {}}, this says \lhd X = \bigoplus _{w \neq \varepsilon } D_w X: earlier is the total derivative.

A grammar assigns to each string w a set B\,{\mathopen {}\left (w\right )\mathclose {}} of parses — a family over strings. Strings with the proper-suffix ordering give a well-founded poset, and thus a direct category. Semantically, the later modality is expressed as {\mathopen {}\left (\rhd B\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (w\right )\mathclose {}} = \prod _{u < w} B\,{\mathopen {}\left (u\right )\mathclose {}}, a product over the proper suffixes of w. An element of \rhd B at w is a B-parse for every strictly shorter remainder of the input: "B, after consuming at least one character". Internal to Dependent Lambek Calculus, we may represent later as \rhd B = \mathchoice {\textstyle \And _{{\mathopen {}\left (w \in \texttt {String}^{+}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \And _{{\mathopen {}\left (w \in \texttt {String}^{+}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \And _{{\mathopen {}\left (w \in \texttt {String}^{+}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \And _{{\mathopen {}\left (w \in \texttt {String}^{+}\right )\mathclose {}}}} \sqrt [w]{B}

fr:title.text: Löb induction on the walking cospan

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction on the walking cospan

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The walking cospan L \to M \leftarrow R is a thin direct category — \mathrm {deg} L = \mathrm {deg} R = 0 and \mathrm {deg} M = 1 — and the smallest one whose past branches. Presheaves on it are spans of sets.
Later at the apex joins the two legs, \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (M\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (L\right )\mathclose {}} \times P{\mathopen {}\left (R\right )\mathclose {}}

fr:title.text: Löb induction on the walking parallel pair

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction on the walking parallel pair

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The walking parallel pair — objects V, E with two parallel morphisms s, t : V \to E — is a direct category with \mathrm {deg} V = 0 and \mathrm {deg} E = 1, the smallest one that is not thin. Presheaves on it are directed multigraphs.

Later computes to \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \top , \qquad \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (E\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}} \times P{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}}, one factor per parallel morphism — a plurality no poset can produce — and \mathsf {next} sends an edge to its pair of endpoints: it is the boundary map.
By naturality, a Löb step \rhd P \to P is a choice of vertex together with a self-loop at it, and its fixed point is that self-loop.

fr:title.text: Unification by Löb induction over thinnings

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Unification by Löb induction over thinnings

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-order unification, following McBride's structurally recursive unification algorithm. The algorithm is built using Löb induction on families over the category of thinnings with the well-founded measure given by size of the scope.
A scope is a list of variable names, and a thinning is an order-preserving embedding of one scope into another, as in McBride's Everybody's Got To Be Somewhere.

Scopes exist to scope terms: over a scope \mathit {xs}, one may define terms for describing a first-order unification algorithm t \;::=\; \mathsf {var}\ x \;\mid \; \mathsf {leaf} \;\mid \; \mathsf {fork}\ t\ t, with x a variable of \mathit {xs}, and a thinning embedding the terms over its source into the terms over its target.

The category is direct with degree given by the length of the scope. It is not thin, as there are two thinnings from {\mathopen {}\left [A\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [A , A\right ]\mathclose {}}.

Define "n is prime" by trial division — dividing only by primes: n is prime when n \ge 2 and no prime p < n divides it. This is a correct characterization but not a structural definition: the predicate at n quantifies over the predicate at every smaller number.

It is, exactly, a guarded definition over \omega . The motive at n is a decided proposition; later on families hands the step the decided propositions of all p < n, and the step forms P_n = {\mathopen {}\left (n \ge 2\right )\mathclose {}} \wedge \forall p < n.\; P_p \to p \nmid n, deciding it from the finitely many earlier decisions and decidability of divisibility.

fr:title.text: Streams in the topos of trees

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Streams in the topos of trees

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Presheaves on \omega — the topos of trees — are the usual setting for this kind of guarded reasoning: step-indexing, later, and Löb in their classical form.

There one solves guarded domain equations. Streams over a set A satisfy \mathrm {Str} \;\cong \; A \times \rhd \mathrm {Str}, and since X \mapsto A \times \rhd X is locally contractive, the equation has a unique solution, and every coalgebra unfolds uniquely.

Given a finite weighted graph, with weights drawn from a sufficiently nice semiring, we compute single-source shortest paths. The analysis is a family of subgraphs of the input, varying in time. Initially nothing is explored beyond the source itself; as time passes, edges are traversed, and each reached vertex is annotated with the shortest path found from the source.

Graphs are presheaves on the walking parallel pair, so time-varying graphs are presheaves on the direct category obtained as the product of the parallel pair with \omega . Löb induction over this base describes the computation.

As written, the recursion is driven by an amount of fuel, which we instantiate with the size of the input graph. This external supply of fuel is a bit unsatisfying, and I wonder if we may hope to internalize things in a better manner in the future.

fr:title.text: The Ackermann function by Löb induction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Ackermann function by Löb induction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Ackermann function A{\mathopen {}\left (0, n\right )\mathclose {}} = n+1, \quad A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = A{\mathopen {}\left (m, 1\right )\mathclose {}}, \quad A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = A{\mathopen {}\left (m,\, A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, n\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} is total but not primitive recursive, and its recursion is not structural: the result of the inner call indexes the outer call.
Löb induction on families over \mathbb {N} \times \mathbb {N} with the lexicographic order shows it terminates.

fr:title.text: Towers of Hanoi by Löb induction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Towers of Hanoi by Löb induction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Move n disks from one peg to another using a third. The functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (0\right )\mathclose {}} = \top , \qquad H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = X{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}} \times X{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, locally contractive because its argument appears only at the strictly smaller stage.

The coalgebra decomposes a problem — pegs labelled source, target, spare — at n+1 into the two rotated subproblems: the tower moves to the spare peg, then onto the relocated largest disk. The algebra joins a pair of move lists around the move of that disk: {\mathopen {}\left (l, r\right )\mathclose {}} \;\mapsto \; l \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [\mathrm {move}\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} r.

The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is the solution; its unfolding is the recurrence \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [\mathrm {move}\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, and taking lengths gives the move count 2^n - 1 by induction.

Multiply x and y using three half-size multiplications instead of four. Split both numbers at a half-width base B, x = x_0 + B \cdot x_1, \qquad y = y_0 + B \cdot y_1. Over pairs of numbers measured by a bound on their digit count, the functor of subproblems requests the three products X{\mathopen {}\left (x_0, y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, \qquad X{\mathopen {}\left (x_1, y_1\right )\mathclose {}}, \qquad X{\mathopen {}\left (x_1 + x_0,\; y_1 + y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, each at a strictly smaller digit bound — locally contractive by that certificate.

The coalgebra performs the split. The motive at {\mathopen {}\left (x, y\right )\mathclose {}} is \sum _{p} {\mathopen {}\left (p = x \cdot y\right )\mathclose {}} — a number with a proof that it is the product — so the recursive results arrive as z_0 = x_0 y_0, \qquad z_2 = x_1 y_1, \qquad z_{11} = {\mathopen {}\left (x_1 + x_0\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (y_1 + y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, each with its proof, and the algebra recombines them through Karatsuba's identity x \cdot y = z_2 \cdot B^2 + {\mathopen {}\left (z_{11} - z_2 - z_0\right )\mathclose {}} \cdot B + z_0, its one proof obligation.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is the multiplier: it evaluates on numerals, correct by the type of its result.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is binary search: on a concrete list it evaluates to a membership witness with its index, or to a refutation.

Sort a finite multiset — so that "same contents" is an equation of bags — following intrinsically correct sorting and Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann. Over bags measured by size, the functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (b\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (b = \varnothing \right )\mathclose {}} \;+\; \sum _{b \,=\, \mathit {lo} + {\mathopen {}\left \{p\right \}\mathclose {}} + \mathit {hi}} X{\mathopen {}\left (\mathit {lo}\right )\mathclose {}} \times X{\mathopen {}\left (\mathit {hi}\right )\mathclose {}}, the sum ranging over pivot decompositions with \mathit {lo} \le p \le \mathit {hi} and \mathit {lo}, \mathit {hi} \prec b.

The coalgebra partitions at a pivot. The algebra concatenates sorted lists, {\mathopen {}\left (l, r\right )\mathclose {}} \;\mapsto \; l \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [p\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} r, spending the bounds at the seams and the recombination equation on the contents.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is quicksort, sorted and content-preserving by the type of its result — and by uniqueness, any function satisfying the recurrence is this one.

Compute the greatest common divisor of m and n, following Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann. Over pairs measured by their second component, the functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = \top , \qquad H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} = X{\mathopen {}\left (n,\, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}} \quad {\mathopen {}\left (n > 0\right )\mathclose {}}, locally contractive because m \bmod n < n.

The coalgebra tests n. The algebra carries the correctness: at {\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} it yields m with a proof that it is a greatest common divisor, and at {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} it transports a gcd of {\mathopen {}\left (n, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}} to one of {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}}.

The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is Euclid's algorithm together with its correctness; its unfolding is \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = m, \qquad \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} = \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (n,\, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}}, and \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (12, 8\right )\mathclose {}} = 4 by evaluation.
em-family — displayed algebra, the adjoint triple, and the (co)monadicity of presheaves over families.

fr:title.text: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}. Write \varepsilon : F \circ U \Rightarrow \mathsf {Id} for the counit of the adjunction. Every object d of \mathcal {D} then induces a T-algebra carried by the object U d, witnessed by the map U \varepsilon _{d} : U F U d \to U d.
This assignment extends to a functor into the Eilenberg–Moore category, K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, the comparison functor of the adjunction.

Dually, an adjunction induces a comonad on the other side and a comparison into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category. When these comparisons are equivalences we say that the adjunction F \dashv U is (co)monadic.

The total category \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} of a displayed category \overline {\mathcal {D}} over \mathcal {C} collects the displayed data into a single category. Its objects are pairs of an object x of \mathcal {C} with an object \overline {x} over it, and its morphisms {\mathopen {}\left (x, \overline {x}\right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (y, \overline {y}\right )\mathclose {}} are pairs of a morphism f : x \to y with a displayed morphism \overline {f} over f.

Projecting out the first components is a functor \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} \to \mathcal {C}. Constructions presented displayed — algebras, Eilenberg–Moore categories — get their forgetful functor for free as this projection.

An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}, and a comparison functor K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} sending each object of \mathcal {D} to the T-algebra it carries.
The functor U is monadic when K is an equivalence: the adjunction exhibits \mathcal {D} as objects of \mathcal {C} equipped with algebraic structure for T, the Eilenberg–Moore category.

Comonadicity is monadicity in the opposite category: a left adjoint L : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} with right adjoint R induces a comonad W = L \circ R on \mathcal {C}, a comparison \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category, and L is comonadic when this comparison is an equivalence.

fr:title.text: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The adjoint triple \mathrm {Free} \dashv U \dashv \mathrm {Cofree} induces a monad T = U \circ \mathrm {Free} and a comonad W = U \circ \mathrm {Cofree} on \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}.

Both comparison functors are equivalences: presheaves are the Eilenberg–Moore algebras of T and the co-Eilenberg–Moore coalgebras of W, \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} \qquad \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}. So presheaves are both monadic and comonadic over families.

Reading the algebra structure concretely: a T-algebra on a family A is a map \sum _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , y\right )\mathclose {}} \times A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each x, subject to the monad algebra laws — that is, exactly a functorial action of restriction.

The comonadic reading is the same structure seen from the element's side: a W-coalgebra is a map A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \to \prod _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (y , x\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, giving each value its restriction along every morphism into x. Where the monad says restriction acts on values, the comonad says a value already carries all of its restrictions — and the coalgebra laws say it does so coherently.

fr:title.text: The adjoint triple between presheaves and families

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The adjoint triple between presheaves and families

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A family over \mathcal {C} is a set A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each object x, with no action of morphisms. Families form a category \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}: a morphism A \to B is a function A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \to B{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each x.

The two adjoints demonstrate different means of forcing a family to be functorial. The right adjoint universally quantifies over morphisms in, \mathrm {Cofree}{\mathopen {}\left (A\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \prod _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (y , x\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, with restriction along f given by precomposition. The left adjoint instead existentially quantifiers over morphisms out: \mathrm {Free}{\mathopen {}\left (A\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \sum _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , y\right )\mathclose {}} \times A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, with restriction acting on the first component. (For \mathrm {Free} we ask that \mathcal {C} have a set of objects, so that this sum is a set and thus \mathrm {Free} defines a presheaf.)

fr:title.text: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A comonad on \mathcal {C} is a monad W on \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}. Everything about its coalgebras is then inherited from the Eilenberg–Moore construction, instantiated at the opposite category — nothing is defined twice.

Algebras of W over \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} are coalgebras \gamma \in \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , W x\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor, and the monad algebra laws, read in \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}, are the comonad coalgebra laws — the unit and multiplication of W, viewed in \mathcal {C}, are the counit \varepsilon and comultiplication \delta : \gamma \star \varepsilon _{x} = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \gamma \star \delta _{x} = \gamma \star W \gamma .

The co-Eilenberg–Moore category is the opposite of the total category: \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (\mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}}.

fr:title.text: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Fix a monad {\mathopen {}\left (T, \eta , \mu \right )\mathclose {}} on \mathcal {C}. Its Eilenberg–Moore category arises in two displayed layers. The first layer is the displayed category of algebras \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor.

The second layer, \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, is displayed over the total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}. Over an algebra {\mathopen {}\left (x, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} the displayed objects are the propositions that \alpha satisfies the monad algebra laws: \eta _{x} \star \alpha = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \mu _{x} \star \alpha = T \alpha \star \alpha .
The Eilenberg–Moore category is the total category of the tower: \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}.

Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. A terminal F-coalgebra, written \nu F, is a terminal object of the category of coalgebras \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} — equivalently, an initial algebra for F^{\mathrm {op}}.

Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. Coalgebras require no new construction: a coalgebra is an algebra in the opposite category. Define \mathrm {CoalgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F^{\mathrm {op}}\right )\mathclose {}}, a displayed category over \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}, where F^{\mathrm {op}} : \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} \to \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} is F acting on the opposite category.
Concretely, over an object x a displayed object is a structure map \gamma \in \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , F x\right )\mathclose {}}.

The category of coalgebras is the opposite of the total category: \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (\int \mathrm {CoalgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}}. The outer opposite returns morphisms to the direction of \mathcal {C}: a morphism {\mathopen {}\left (x, \gamma \right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (y, \delta \right )\mathclose {}} is a map f : x \to y with f \star \delta = \gamma \star F f.
Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. An initial F-algebra, written \mu F, is an initial object of the category of algebras \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}.
Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. The F-algebras form a displayed category \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} over \mathcal {C}.
The total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} is the category of F-algebras.
lob-ind — direct categories, the guarded modalities, Löb induction, and the recursion theorems.

A predecessor of x is a top element of its strict downset: a strict morphism \rho : p \to x through which every strict morphism into x factors uniquely. Equivalently, \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \mathsf {y}\, p — the downset is representable.

The Yoneda lemma then collapses later to evaluation: \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}},\, P\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {y}\, p,\, P\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (p\right )\mathclose {}}, with \mathsf {next} becoming restriction along \rho . The name is from the naturals: every strict map into n+1 factors through n \to n+1, so on \omega — the topos of trees — later is just the shift \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (0\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \top , \qquad \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, and a Löb step is a base value together with a rule producing the value at n+1 from the value at n.
When this predecessor exists, we can give a simpler description of later, as in the topos of trees, but this may not be possible in all direct categories.

fr:title.text: Fixed points of locally contractive functors are unique

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Fixed points of locally contractive functors are unique

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let F be a locally contractive endofunctor on presheaves over a direct category, and suppose X is any fixed point of F: an object with an isomorphism \alpha : F X \cong X. Then {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} is an initial algebra \mu F, and simultaneously {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha ^{-1}\right )\mathclose {}} is a terminal coalgebra \nu F.

Both claims follow from every value of the hylomorphism profunctor being a singleton. For initiality of {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}}: a map to an algebra {\mathopen {}\left (B, \beta \right )\mathclose {}} satisfying the initiality square is the same thing as a hylomorphism from the coalgebra {\mathopen {}\left (X, \alpha ^{-1}\right )\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left (B, \beta \right )\mathclose {}}, and there is exactly one of those. Terminality follows similarly.
Thus for locally contractive F, we have an inital-final coincidence \mu F \;\cong \; \nu F, and guarded domain equations have unique solutions.

fr:title.text: Local contractivity makes every coalgebra recursive

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Local contractivity makes every coalgebra recursive

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If F is a locally contractive endofunctor on presheaves over a direct category, then every value of the profunctor \mathrm {Hylo} is a singleton: every coalgebra is recursive and every algebra is corecursive.

The proof is one application of Löb. A hylomorphism from c to \alpha is a fixed point of h \mapsto c \star F h \star \alpha on the presheaf X \Rightarrow B of morphisms. Local contractivity factors this assignment through \rhd {\mathopen {}\left (X \Rightarrow B\right )\mathclose {}}, so Löb provides a fixed point and provides it uniquely.

Hylomorphisms organize into a profunctor \mathrm {Hylo} : \mathrm {Coalg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}} \times \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathbf {Set}, sending a coalgebra and an algebra to the set of hylomorphisms between them.

A coalgebra is recursive exactly when \mathrm {Hylo}{\mathopen {}\left (c, -\right )\mathclose {}} is constantly a singleton, and an algebra is corecursive exactly when \mathrm {Hylo}{\mathopen {}\left (-, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} is. Saying that all values of \mathrm {Hylo} are singletons says every divide-and-conquer specification over F has a unique solution.

A coalgebra c : X \to F X is recursive when for every algebra \alpha : F B \to B there is exactly one hylomorphism X \to B. Recursion out of X is then total and deterministic: any way of recombining answers yields a uniquely-defined function.

Recursiveness is the coalgebraic form of well-foundedness. The coalgebra decomposes each element into subproblems; recursiveness says this decomposition terminates, without ever mentioning an ordering. Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann develop intrinsically correct algorithms from exactly this notion.

Dually, an algebra \alpha is corecursive when every coalgebra admits exactly one hylomorphism into it. An initial algebra gives a recursive coalgebra (its inverse, by Lambek's lemma), and a terminal coalgebra gives a corecursive algebra.
This is divide and conquer as an equation: c splits a problem into an F-shape of subproblems, F h solves them, and \alpha recombines the answers.
Just as for presheaves, the fixed point constructed above is unique: the two transpositions are bijections, and the presheaf-level fixed point is already unique.

Conjugation with the adjunction between presheaves and families U \dashv \mathrm {Cofree} lets us induce a later construction on families from the one on presheaves, \rhd _{\mathrm {Fam}} = U \circ \rhd \circ \mathrm {Cofree} : \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}.

Write X \Rightarrow Y for the presheaf of morphisms X \to Y. An endofunctor F on presheaves is locally contractive when its action on morphisms factors through later: there is a map F_\delta : \rhd {\mathopen {}\left (X \Rightarrow Y\right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (F X \Rightarrow F Y\right )\mathclose {}}

fr:title.text: Löb induction for presheaves on a direct category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction for presheaves on a direct category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {C} be a direct category and P a presheaf on it. Every map \varphi : \rhd P \to P has a fixed point: a global element \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi : \top \to P with \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi = \mathsf {l\ddot {o}b}\,\varphi \star \mathsf {next} \star \varphi , and this fixed point is unique.

The hypothesis says: the value of P at any object is determined by its values over the strict past — \varphi turns a coherent family over the strict downset of x into a value at x. The proof is recursion along the well-founded \prec : at each x, the section already constructed over the past assembles into an element of {\mathopen {}\left (\rhd P\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}, and \varphi extends it to x.

Later takes a limit over smaller indices. Dually, the earlier modality takes a colimit over larger indices: an element of \lhd P at x is a P-element sitting at some object y strictly above x, carried down along a chosen morphism x \to y.
Earlier is left adjoint to later: \lhd \dashv \rhd
Under this adjunction, \mathsf {next} : P \to \rhd P corresponds to \mathsf {prev} : \lhd P \to P,.

The later modality on presheaves is given at each object by mapping out of the strict downset: {\mathopen {}\left (\rhd P\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}},\, P\right )\mathclose {}}. An element of \rhd P at x is a coherent choice of P-elements for all objects strictly smaller than x.

Restriction in \rhd P along f : y \to x precomposes with the induced map \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}. At an object of minimal degree the strict downset is empty, so \rhd P is trivial there.

Via functoriality, every presheaf restricts to smaller indices: the map \mathsf {next} : P \to \rhd P sends an element p over x to the family of all its restrictions along morphisms from strictly lower objects.

fr:title.text: Maximality of the strict downset among proper sieves

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Maximality of the strict downset among proper sieves

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Call a direct structure reflecting when every morphism between objects of equal degree is a split epimorphism. In a reflecting direct category, every non-invertible-in-degree morphism strictly raises degree, and the strict downset is as large as a proper sieve can be:
If the direct structure is reflecting, then every proper sieve S on x refines into the strict downset: S \subseteq \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}.

For suppose S \ni f with f : y \to x of equal degree. By reflection f has a section s, and closure under precomposition gives S \ni s \star f = \mathsf {id}_{x}, contradicting properness. So every member of S strictly raises degree, which is membership in \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: The strict downset sieve of a direct category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The strict downset sieve of a direct category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {C} carry a direct structure. The strict downset \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} of an object x is the presheaf of morphisms into x from strictly lower objects: \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}}\,{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left \{f : y \to x \mid y \prec x\right \}\mathclose {}}, with restriction by precomposition — well defined since degrees are non-decreasing, so precomposing can only stay strictly below.

The evident inclusion \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \rightarrowtail よ\, x makes \mathsf {StrictDown}{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} a sieve on x. It is moreover a proper sieve, as it exlcudes the identity.
The representable \mathsf {y}\, x is itself a sieve on x. A sieve on x is proper when it is not equal to the representable.
Say that a proper sieve is maximal when it contains all other proper sieves as a sub-sieve.

A sieve on an object x of \mathcal {C} is a subobject of the representable presheaf \mathsf {y}\, x: a presheaf S with a monic morphism S \rightarrowtail \mathsf {y}\, x. Sieves are a generalization from the notion of ideal found in ring theory to category theory.

A morphism f : y 	o x belongs to S, written S 
i f, when f lies in the image of the inclusion at y. Because S is a presheaf and the inclusion is natural, membership is closed under precomposition: S 
i f \Rightarrow S 
i g \star f \quad 	ext {for every } g : z 	o y. A sieve is thus a "downward closed" collection of morphisms into x.
Sieves on x are ordered by refinement: S \subseteq T when every morphism belonging to S belongs to T.

fr:title.text: Well-founded posets are thin direct categories

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Well-founded posets are thin direct categories

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every poset forms a thin category. Similarly, if the poset is well-founded then it induces a thin direct category.

A well-founded order is a set D with a proposition-valued transitive relation < admitting no infinite descent: every element is accessible. Write a \le b for {\mathopen {}\left (a < b\right )\mathclose {}} \vee {\mathopen {}\left (a = b\right )\mathclose {}}.

A direct structure on a category \mathcal {C} over {\mathopen {}\left (D, <\right )\mathclose {}} is a functor \mathrm {deg} : \mathcal {C} \to {\mathopen {}\left (D, \le \right )\mathclose {}} into the well-founded order viewed as a poset category. The functor organizes two pieces of data at once: an ordering on the objects, and the invariant that morphisms respect it — f : x \to y forces \mathrm {deg} x \le \mathrm {deg} y.
lob-examples — instances and verified algorithms.

An ordered semiring is a set with two monoid structures: an idempotent, commutative choice \oplus with unit \mathbb {0}, and a combination \otimes with unit \mathbb {1}, distributing over choice and annihilated by \mathbb {0}. Idempotent choice induces an order, x \sqsubseteq y \iff x \oplus y = x, under which \oplus is the meet and \mathbb {0} the top element.

It is selective when choice always takes one of its arguments: x \oplus y = x \quad \text {or}\quad x \oplus y = y. Selectivity is what makes optima witnessed: a value assembled by repeated choice is literally one of the candidates offered, so a certificate for the chosen candidate is a certificate for the result.

For shortest paths, read \oplus as "take the better route", \otimes as "extend a route by an edge", \mathbb {0} as unreachable and \mathbb {1} as the free route. Min-plus on costs gives shortest distances; the Boolean semiring (or, and) gives reachability.

fr:title.text: Löb induction on the walking cospan

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction on the walking cospan

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The walking cospan L \to M \leftarrow R is a thin direct category — \mathrm {deg} L = \mathrm {deg} R = 0 and \mathrm {deg} M = 1 — and the smallest one whose past branches. Presheaves on it are spans of sets.
Later at the apex joins the two legs, \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (M\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (L\right )\mathclose {}} \times P{\mathopen {}\left (R\right )\mathclose {}}

fr:title.text: Löb induction on the walking parallel pair

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Löb induction on the walking parallel pair

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The walking parallel pair — objects V, E with two parallel morphisms s, t : V \to E — is a direct category with \mathrm {deg} V = 0 and \mathrm {deg} E = 1, the smallest one that is not thin. Presheaves on it are directed multigraphs.

Later computes to \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}} \cong \top , \qquad \rhd P\,{\mathopen {}\left (E\right )\mathclose {}} \cong P{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}} \times P{\mathopen {}\left (V\right )\mathclose {}}, one factor per parallel morphism — a plurality no poset can produce — and \mathsf {next} sends an edge to its pair of endpoints: it is the boundary map.
By naturality, a Löb step \rhd P \to P is a choice of vertex together with a self-loop at it, and its fixed point is that self-loop.

fr:title.text: Unification by Löb induction over thinnings

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Unification by Löb induction over thinnings

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-order unification, following McBride's structurally recursive unification algorithm. The algorithm is built using Löb induction on families over the category of thinnings with the well-founded measure given by size of the scope.
A scope is a list of variable names, and a thinning is an order-preserving embedding of one scope into another, as in McBride's Everybody's Got To Be Somewhere.

Scopes exist to scope terms: over a scope \mathit {xs}, one may define terms for describing a first-order unification algorithm t \;::=\; \mathsf {var}\ x \;\mid \; \mathsf {leaf} \;\mid \; \mathsf {fork}\ t\ t, with x a variable of \mathit {xs}, and a thinning embedding the terms over its source into the terms over its target.

The category is direct with degree given by the length of the scope. It is not thin, as there are two thinnings from {\mathopen {}\left [A\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [A , A\right ]\mathclose {}}.

Define "n is prime" by trial division — dividing only by primes: n is prime when n \ge 2 and no prime p < n divides it. This is a correct characterization but not a structural definition: the predicate at n quantifies over the predicate at every smaller number.

It is, exactly, a guarded definition over \omega . The motive at n is a decided proposition; later on families hands the step the decided propositions of all p < n, and the step forms P_n = {\mathopen {}\left (n \ge 2\right )\mathclose {}} \wedge \forall p < n.\; P_p \to p \nmid n, deciding it from the finitely many earlier decisions and decidability of divisibility.

fr:title.text: Streams in the topos of trees

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Streams in the topos of trees

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Presheaves on \omega — the topos of trees — are the usual setting for this kind of guarded reasoning: step-indexing, later, and Löb in their classical form.

There one solves guarded domain equations. Streams over a set A satisfy \mathrm {Str} \;\cong \; A \times \rhd \mathrm {Str}, and since X \mapsto A \times \rhd X is locally contractive, the equation has a unique solution, and every coalgebra unfolds uniquely.

Given a finite weighted graph, with weights drawn from a sufficiently nice semiring, we compute single-source shortest paths. The analysis is a family of subgraphs of the input, varying in time. Initially nothing is explored beyond the source itself; as time passes, edges are traversed, and each reached vertex is annotated with the shortest path found from the source.

Graphs are presheaves on the walking parallel pair, so time-varying graphs are presheaves on the direct category obtained as the product of the parallel pair with \omega . Löb induction over this base describes the computation.

As written, the recursion is driven by an amount of fuel, which we instantiate with the size of the input graph. This external supply of fuel is a bit unsatisfying, and I wonder if we may hope to internalize things in a better manner in the future.

fr:title.text: The Ackermann function by Löb induction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Ackermann function by Löb induction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Ackermann function A{\mathopen {}\left (0, n\right )\mathclose {}} = n+1, \quad A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = A{\mathopen {}\left (m, 1\right )\mathclose {}}, \quad A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = A{\mathopen {}\left (m,\, A{\mathopen {}\left (m+1, n\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} is total but not primitive recursive, and its recursion is not structural: the result of the inner call indexes the outer call.
Löb induction on families over \mathbb {N} \times \mathbb {N} with the lexicographic order shows it terminates.

fr:title.text: Towers of Hanoi by Löb induction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Towers of Hanoi by Löb induction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Move n disks from one peg to another using a third. The functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (0\right )\mathclose {}} = \top , \qquad H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = X{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}} \times X{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, locally contractive because its argument appears only at the strictly smaller stage.

The coalgebra decomposes a problem — pegs labelled source, target, spare — at n+1 into the two rotated subproblems: the tower moves to the spare peg, then onto the relocated largest disk. The algebra joins a pair of move lists around the move of that disk: {\mathopen {}\left (l, r\right )\mathclose {}} \;\mapsto \; l \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [\mathrm {move}\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} r.

The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is the solution; its unfolding is the recurrence \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n+1\right )\mathclose {}} = \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [\mathrm {move}\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} \mathrm {hanoi}\,{\mathopen {}\left (n\right )\mathclose {}}, and taking lengths gives the move count 2^n - 1 by induction.

Multiply x and y using three half-size multiplications instead of four. Split both numbers at a half-width base B, x = x_0 + B \cdot x_1, \qquad y = y_0 + B \cdot y_1. Over pairs of numbers measured by a bound on their digit count, the functor of subproblems requests the three products X{\mathopen {}\left (x_0, y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, \qquad X{\mathopen {}\left (x_1, y_1\right )\mathclose {}}, \qquad X{\mathopen {}\left (x_1 + x_0,\; y_1 + y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, each at a strictly smaller digit bound — locally contractive by that certificate.

The coalgebra performs the split. The motive at {\mathopen {}\left (x, y\right )\mathclose {}} is \sum _{p} {\mathopen {}\left (p = x \cdot y\right )\mathclose {}} — a number with a proof that it is the product — so the recursive results arrive as z_0 = x_0 y_0, \qquad z_2 = x_1 y_1, \qquad z_{11} = {\mathopen {}\left (x_1 + x_0\right )\mathclose {}}{\mathopen {}\left (y_1 + y_0\right )\mathclose {}}, each with its proof, and the algebra recombines them through Karatsuba's identity x \cdot y = z_2 \cdot B^2 + {\mathopen {}\left (z_{11} - z_2 - z_0\right )\mathclose {}} \cdot B + z_0, its one proof obligation.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is the multiplier: it evaluates on numerals, correct by the type of its result.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is binary search: on a concrete list it evaluates to a membership witness with its index, or to a refutation.

Sort a finite multiset — so that "same contents" is an equation of bags — following intrinsically correct sorting and Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann. Over bags measured by size, the functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (b\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (b = \varnothing \right )\mathclose {}} \;+\; \sum _{b \,=\, \mathit {lo} + {\mathopen {}\left \{p\right \}\mathclose {}} + \mathit {hi}} X{\mathopen {}\left (\mathit {lo}\right )\mathclose {}} \times X{\mathopen {}\left (\mathit {hi}\right )\mathclose {}}, the sum ranging over pivot decompositions with \mathit {lo} \le p \le \mathit {hi} and \mathit {lo}, \mathit {hi} \prec b.

The coalgebra partitions at a pivot. The algebra concatenates sorted lists, {\mathopen {}\left (l, r\right )\mathclose {}} \;\mapsto \; l \mathbin {++} {\mathopen {}\left [p\right ]\mathclose {}} \mathbin {++} r, spending the bounds at the seams and the recombination equation on the contents.
The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is quicksort, sorted and content-preserving by the type of its result — and by uniqueness, any function satisfying the recurrence is this one.

Compute the greatest common divisor of m and n, following Alexandru, Urbat, and Wißmann. Over pairs measured by their second component, the functor of subproblems is H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = \top , \qquad H X\,{\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} = X{\mathopen {}\left (n,\, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}} \quad {\mathopen {}\left (n > 0\right )\mathclose {}}, locally contractive because m \bmod n < n.

The coalgebra tests n. The algebra carries the correctness: at {\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} it yields m with a proof that it is a greatest common divisor, and at {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} it transports a gcd of {\mathopen {}\left (n, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}} to one of {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}}.

The hylomorphism of this coalgebra and algebra is Euclid's algorithm together with its correctness; its unfolding is \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (m, 0\right )\mathclose {}} = m, \qquad \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (m, n\right )\mathclose {}} = \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (n,\, m \bmod n\right )\mathclose {}}, and \gcd {\mathopen {}\left (12, 8\right )\mathclose {}} = 4 by evaluation.
